main_summary.summary: Reginald Robinson, born October 2, 1939 in Baltimore, recounts his trajectory from Cortez Peters Business College student to SNCC field secretary, interviewed by Emilye Crosby on December 11, 2015 with John Bishop at the Library of Congress. Robinson describes joining Baltimore's Civic Interest Group under Dean Walter Dixon in 1960, becoming its treasurer, and in June 1961 taking Clarence Mitchell's open Maryland SNCC seat after Mitchell withdrew under family NAACP pressure. He details his identification by Ella Baker on Anne Braden's porch in Louisville and his August 1961 assignment to McComb, Mississippi, where he worked alongside Bob Moses, opened bank and stationery accounts in SNCC's name, set up the voter-registration school over Burglund Supermarket, and visited jailed students Curtis Hayes and Hollis Watkins in the Magnolia jail. Robinson narrates the day Bob Moses, Chuck McDew, and others were beaten outside the McComb courthouse following Herbert Lee's killing by State Representative E.H. Hurst, and his protection of Diane Nash during the police sweep of the Masonic Temple. He then describes founding-period SNCC work in Crisfield and Cambridge, Maryland with Bill Hansen and Gloria Richardson, voter-registration work in Orangeburg, MFDP fundraising in Atlantic City 1964, and his post-1965 career running UPO Center #1, the Child Development Group of Mississippi, OSTI projects in Detroit and Oakland with the Black Panthers and Kaiser Engineers, polling work for Marion Barry's first DC campaign through Afro-American Data Namix on a DEC PDP-11/45, and twenty years at the DC Department of Employment Services through May 2001.
key_themes: SNCC field organizing and the 1961 McComb voter-registration project
Baltimore Civic Interest Group and the public-accommodations sit-in campaign
Cambridge, Maryland movement and Gloria Richardson's leadership
Ella Baker's mentorship and bottom-up organizing philosophy
Post-movement careers in federal poverty programming and Black economic development
main_summary.historical_significance:
Robinson is one of the original sixteen SNCC field secretaries dispatched in summer 1961 and the first SNCC worker on the ground in McComb alongside Bob Moses, making his first-person testimony a primary source for the McComb voter-registration project, the killing of Herbert Lee, and the SNCC-SCLC strategic rivalry at the August 1961 Baltimore meeting. His account documents the organizational mechanics rarely captured elsewhere: how SNCC opened business accounts in Southern towns, how mass-meeting collections were taken before SCLC clergy arrived, how George Hitch and 'Super Cool Daddy' Mr. Webb provided local infrastructure, and how George Gelston and the Maryland National Guard negotiated with movement organizers in Cambridge.

The interview also provides a rare bridge between the 1961-1965 SNCC field-secretary era and the post-movement diaspora into federal anti-poverty work (UPO, CDGM under Senator Stennis's hostility), Oakland Black Panther-adjacent housing development with Kaiser Engineers, early Black political polling (Marion Barry's first DC campaign on a DEC PDP-11/45), and DC government employment work. Robinson's closing economic argument that voter registration alone is insufficient and that Black economic infrastructure (the laundromat, the financial institution on the corner, denim manufacturing like Red Brown's Mace) is the next-generation movement task represents the distinctive SNCC-veteran political analysis of the 2010s.

chapters.title: Baltimore Childhood and Cortez Peters
chapters.summary: Robinson recounts his Baltimore birth in October 1939, parents Leroy and Leovester Jenkins Robinson, his father's 39 years at Bethlehem Steel, and dropping out of Dunbar High School around tenth grade. His cousin Josephine Thorn convinces him to enroll at Cortez Peters Business College, founded by a Black typist who learned 150 words per minute and was financed by Royal or Underwood Typewriter to teach Black students government-test skills and shorthand in DC, Baltimore, and Chicago.

chapters.title: Civic Interest Group and Voter Registration
chapters.summary: After the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, Robinson is pushed by Dean Walter Dixon, simultaneously his college dean and a Baltimore City Councilman who introduced a public-accommodations bill, to represent the school in the Civic Interest Group. He becomes treasurer, runs sound-truck voter-registration drives in Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s district that register approximately 30,000 people, and learns to handle accounting under union representative Troy Brailey.

chapters.title: SNCC Seat and Ella Baker
chapters.summary: When Clarence Mitchell withdraws under family NAACP pressure, Robinson takes Maryland's open SNCC seat in June 1961 and travels to Louisville for the SNCC meeting wearing a black eye patch from a pretzel injury. On Anne Braden's porch, Ella Baker identifies him by tracing his mother Leovester Jenkins back to his uncle McKinley, who worked with her in New York. Baker's recognition opens him into the SNCC inner circle and a Baltimore SNCC mass meeting where the collection is taken before Wyatt Tee Walker can claim it for SCLC.

chapters.title: Arrival in McComb
chapters.summary: Robinson travels from Baltimore through Atlanta, where SNCC executive director Ed King introduces him to a tuxedo-clad Wyatt Tee Walker, then to New Orleans and via Illinois Central to McComb. Cab driver George Hitch transports him to C.C. Bryant's house where he meets Bob Moses. With local infrastructure figure 'Super Cool Daddy' Mr. Webb, he arranges housing with Mama Cotton on Wall Street, opens a SNCC bank account, rents a mimeograph at the stationery store, and sets up the voter-registration school in the Masonic Temple over Burglund Supermarket above Burglund High School.

chapters.title: Magnolia Jail and the Constitution Test
chapters.summary: Robinson describes the Mississippi voter-registration mechanics: registrars demanding interpretation of arbitrary sections of the state constitution, SNCC's literacy-school response, and the principle from Bob Moses that organizers must walk applicants to the registrar's office. He recounts Bob Moses's beating, Herbert Lee's killing in the cotton gin by State Representative E.H. Hurst, and his own black-suited visit to the Magnolia jail with George Hitch's concealed silver-handled pistol to see Curtis Hayes and Hollis Watkins.

chapters.title: Baltimore Sit-Ins and Family Support
chapters.summary: Robinson contrasts the relatively non-violent Baltimore sit-in tactics, in which three Renaults full of demonstrators rotated between major restaurants triggering Miranda readings and police closures, with the rougher Mississippi work. He describes the support of William 'Little Willie' Adams who underwrote bail through the Dixon bond agency, Elizabeth Murphy Oliver of the Baltimore Afro-American newspaper and her daughter Marcia, and Larry Steele, the Jet/Ebony stringer who relayed news to his mother during his Southern assignments.

chapters.title: McComb High School Walkout and Protecting Diane Nash
chapters.summary: Robinson narrates the day McComb students walked out of school following Herbert Lee's killing. He observed from a vantage point as police beat Bob Moses, Chuck McDew, and others, then watched from the Masonic Temple as Sherrod and Cordell Reagon were arrested. With a Detroit-based A.G. Gaston and Diane Nash inside, he led them through a black-curtained section onto the roof to evade the sergeant searching the building. Gaston jumped off the roof and was caught; Robinson got Diane Nash safely back to Wall Street.

chapters.title: Crisfield Arrest and Eastern Shore Maryland
chapters.summary: After the retreat to Atlanta and reassignment at BB Beaman's, Robinson coordinates Route 40 desegregation with CORE in late 1961. Ruby Doris Smith Robinson directs him to a CORE group from New York including Faith Holsaert, Peggy Dammond, Angela Butler, and Bill Hansen, who travel to Crisfield, Maryland to sit in at Governor Tawes's restaurant during Christmas 1961. Robinson is arrested for the only time in his six SNCC years; the group is jailed in Princess Anne, Maryland with Juanita Mitchell as attorney and Freddie St. Clair from Cambridge providing bonds.

chapters.title: Cambridge and Gloria Richardson
chapters.summary: Robinson and Bill Hansen organize Cambridge, Maryland with weekend mobilizations from Civic Interest Group and Nonviolent Action Group affiliates. When Freddie St. Clair steps back to run his funeral business, his cousin Gloria Richardson assumes leadership of an organization that negotiates with the National Guard and the Kennedy administration. Robinson confronts Richardson over her call to boycott the public-accommodations referendum, arguing that no SNCC affiliate could advocate non-voting while Southern workers died for the franchise.

chapters.title: Mississippi Reflections and John Doar
chapters.summary: Robinson reflects on Amzie Moore as the post-office veteran NAACP figure who convinced the SNCC team to remain in Mississippi after Herbert Lee's funeral, when their first instinct was to flee. He recounts night affidavit-collection in an open Mississippi field with Bob Moses, John Doar, Marion Barry, and George Hitch, the rural Black farmer's fear that the white folks 'might turn green and jump on you,' and John Doar's coded refusal to acknowledge a beaten John Hardy in a jail cell to protect his cover.

chapters.title: Cambridge National Guard and George Gelston
chapters.summary: After Robinson's Orangeburg South Carolina VEP project, the National Guard under General George Gelston enters Cambridge and becomes an unexpected ally. Gelston attempts to recruit Robinson as an officer and is surprised to confirm Robinson's 4-F mental-incompetent status (Robinson refused to identify inkblots as anything other than inkblots). When Gelston is absent, Colonel Tawes's troops shoot tear gas after Cliff Voss charges fixed bayonets; Stokely Carmichael and others are arrested. Robinson confronts Bobby Kennedy on the Cambridge water situation and on John Doar's reports.

chapters.title: Baltimore Advisory Network and the March on Washington
chapters.summary: Robinson sketches the Baltimore adult-advisory network: August Meier teaching at Morgan State, Reverend Marion Bascom, Reverend Vernon Dobson, Reverend Frank Williams, and Catherine Adams as the advisory-group secretary to whom all records flowed. He recalls the March on Washington as a day of singing and good food, with no assigned role, and his anger at the bumping of SNCC's intended speech content. He describes meeting Diane Nash for the first time across a table in a hotel conference room with Connie Curry catching him doodling a heart between their names.

chapters.title: MFDP Atlantic City and SNCC Fundraising
chapters.summary: In 1964 Robinson briefly works for Senator Joseph D. Tydings before being assigned to lobby Western state delegations for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge in California and Oregon. He is then sent to Atlantic City for the convention, where with NAG's Mary Lovelace O'Neal he secures hotel rooms through Atlantic City gangster Reggie Edgel. He moves to New York for special-gift fundraising, then to the Philadelphia office until Fred Mealy disrupts the operation. Late 1965 finds him surveying SNCC's projects in disarray.

chapters.title: UPO, CDGM, and Senator Stennis
chapters.summary: After leaving SNCC in 1965, Robinson serves as Meg Due's second-in-command at United Planning Organization Center #1 in DC for about a year before being politically pushed out. Ed Brown recruits him as Social Service Director for the Child Development Group of Mississippi. In a meeting during CDGM's dissolution, Senator John C. Stennis repeatedly addresses Robinson's team as 'you boys'; Robinson confronts him directly, and is fired.

chapters.title: OSTI, Kaiser, and the Black Panthers in Oakland
chapters.summary: Through Peggy Dammond's husband, Robinson joins OSTI in Boston. After a Detroit free-Detroit-Committee community project, he is sent to Oakland to broker a 40-year land-acquisition partnership between Kaiser Engineers and the Black Panthers for the Acorn redevelopment in East Oakland. The Panthers refuse, citing his arrival in a Cadillac. Robinson switches the partnership to a community group brokered via Timothy L. Jenkins's contact Don Warden, and the West Project Equal senior-citizens housing is built. He is excluded from white planning meetings and quits.

chapters.title: Back Pain, Marriage End, and Memphis
chapters.summary: Diagnosed with muscle-spasm back pain requiring six hours of daily medicated sleep, Robinson instead quits smoking cigarettes after three days and stops coffee. Bobby Yancey calls him to a Memphis project with A.L. Nellum & Associates organizing the first Congressional Black Caucus dinners. He returns to Boston to find his wife leaving, and relocates to DC in 1981 to take over the project there.

chapters.title: DC Polling, Marion Barry, and Federal Career
chapters.summary: Robinson becomes involved in the DC Charter campaign with John A. Wilson. At Afro-American Data Namix at 14th and Kenyon, on the only Black-owned DEC PDP-11/45 between DC and New York, he runs Marion Barry's first polling operation. After unemployment and Pride work, he joins DC Department of Employment Services running the youth division for two summer-jobs programs (40,000 placements one summer, 25,000 the school year), then becomes a fraud investigator, then assistant LAN administrator overseeing 100 machines. He retires on the day he files papers after a forced incentive-speaker mandatory event.

chapters.title: Albany Jail, Ella Baker, and Paul Robeson
chapters.summary: Robinson recounts riding to Albany jail with Wyatt Tee Walker to deliver pork chops to King and Abernathy, who sat in silk pajamas while Robinson refused to bring Abernathy ice cream. He describes Ella Baker as a maternal figure who took him drunk before his wedding at Harry Belafonte's house. When Cambridge work overwhelmed him, Baker sent him via psychiatrist Joe Baker of the Medical Committee for Human Rights to Peekskill, New York for a Paul Robeson commemorative fundraiser, where he met Paul Robeson at Dr. Samuel and Helen Rosen's Katonah home and sat with him in a sauna.

chapters.title: SNCC Building Strategy and the NAACP Conflict
chapters.summary: Robinson articulates the SNCC organizing principle drawn from Ella Baker: enter a community to build local infrastructure and leave, never to make a home there. He plants the 'silk suits vs. denim' story with reporter Karl Rogoff to expose the NAACP's collection-taking in Cambridge, chasing them out of town. He distinguishes SNCC's pushed-envelope strategy from the NAACP's court-reliant approach and reflects on Baltimore's Freddie Gray uprising and the unchanged poverty in West Baltimore alongside Pikesville's wealth.

chapters.title: Black Power Reframed and Economic Strategy
chapters.summary: Robinson argues that the SNCC move to Black Power was never about guns but about economic infrastructure: a financial institution on a Greenwood Mississippi corner that could loan to a laundromat, a restaurant, a computer-assembly factory leveraging tooling that no longer requires a PhD. He cites Henry G. Parks Jr's Parks Sausage Company, Red Brown's Mace denim manufacturing, and Victorine Q. Adams as Black entrepreneurial precedents. He critiques the 2014 Selma film's misrepresentation of James Forman and SNCC, and emphasizes that SNCC's enduring contribution is the leaderless-leader model.
